content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Self-Debiasing Prompts for Reducing Social Bias in LLMs",
  "problem": "Current LLMs often generate outputs reflecting social biases and stereotypes, even when explicitly prompted for neutrality. Existing prompt engineering and instruction-tuning techniques fail to reliably and robustly mitigate these effects on diverse topics.",
  "motivation": "Mitigating social bias in LLMs is crucial for ethical, fair, and responsible AI deployment, especially as models see widespread use in sensitive contexts. Prompting methods are appealing because they require no model retraining and can be flexibly deployed. Yet, most prompt interventions to date yield only marginal improvements and are brittle across context shifts. A breakthrough in effective prompt-based debiasing could shift how practitioners address bias mitigation.",
  "method": "We propose 'Counterfactual Self-Debiasing Prompts': for any user query, the LLM is prompted to answer twice—once as usual, and once as if it came from the perspective of a counterfactual identity group (e.g., differing in gender, race, or religion). The model is then prompted to compare the two outputs and explicitly revise its initial response to remove differences attributable to social bias, with instructions to preserve only content differences grounded in the query context. This self-contrastive prompting method guides the model to surface and mitigate its own stereotyped inferences in generation-time.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate on the CrowS-Pairs, StereoSet, and Winogender datasets, measuring bias metrics (e.g., bias score, stereotype agreement rate) and task utility (fluency, informativeness). Baselines include standard prompts, debiasing instruction prompts, and prompt-chaining. Human evaluation will assess stereotype presence and output acceptability."
}
